SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The experiments are solid: the model is evaluated on the Belinkov et al. PP attachment dataset with strong baselines (GloVe, GloVe-retro, and the HPCD system), reports clear accuracy/UAS gains (5.4 pp / 34.4% relative error reduction), and provides hyperparameters and code for reproducibility. However, evaluation is limited to a single English PP dataset, no ablation or sensitivity analyses are reported in the summary, and the paper does not discuss failure cases or limitations. Given the strong baselines and reproducibility but these analysis gaps, the work merits a score of 4. | B(3): The experiments show clear gains on the Belinkov et al. (2014) PP attachment dataset and compare against reasonable baselines (LSTM-PP with GloVe and GloVe-retro, and HPCD), and the authors release code and hyperparameters which supports reproducibility. However, evaluation is limited to a single dataset/task and no ablation or sensitivity analyses are reported to diagnose which components drive the gains. Related work coverage is described as thorough, but the paper does not discuss failure cases or limitations. Overall, the empirical work is adequate to support the main claim on this dataset but misses important analyses and broader evaluation.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper proposes a notable and specific new idea: modeling a token as a context-sensitive probability distribution over WordNet synsets and using the expected synset embedding as the token representation, which goes beyond type-level embeddings and retrofitting. Integrating this OntoLSTM token representation with bi-LSTM encoders for PP attachment and demonstrating a substantial accuracy gain shows a non-trivial technical extension. The formulation targets a well-studied task (PP attachment), so the contribution is an applied methodological advance rather than a wholly new problem, but it is sufficiently differentiated from prior work to be of clear interest to experts. | B(4): The paper introduces a notable novel method: modeling each word token as a context-sensitive probability distribution over WordNet synsets and using the expected synset embedding as the token representation, which goes beyond static type-level or retrofitted embeddings. It clearly differentiates this approach from prior work (e.g., GloVe, GloVe-retro) and integrates it with bi-LSTM+MLP for PP attachment, demonstrating substantial empirical gains on the Belinkov et al. dataset; this constitutes a non-trivial and informative extension rather than a mere reapplication of known techniques.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes a notable and specific new idea: modeling a token as a context-sensitive probability distribution over WordNet synsets and using the expected synset embedding as the token representation, which goes beyond type-level embeddings and retrofitting. Integrating this OntoLSTM token representation with bi-LSTM encoders for PP attachment and demonstrating a substantial accuracy gain shows a non-trivial technical extension. reviewer_b.justification: The paper introduces a notable novel method: modeling each word token as a context-sensitive probability distribution over WordNet synsets and using the expected synset embedding as the token representation, which goes beyond static type-level or retrofitted embeddings. It clearly differentiates this approach from prior work (e.g., GloVe, GloVe-retro) and integrates it with bi-LSTM+MLP for PP attachment, demonstrating substantial empirical gains on the Belinkov et al. dataset; this constitutes a non-trivial and informative extension rather than a mere reapplication of known techniques.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles the important problem of PP attachment disambiguation and proposes a concrete method—WordNet-grounded context-sensitive token embeddings—integrated into a BiLSTM+MLP model. Empirically it yields a substantial gain (5.4 percentage points, a 34.4% relative error reduction) over standard type-level embeddings on the Belinkov et al. dataset, showing that ontology grounding can materially improve this syntactic task. By releasing Keras layers and demonstrating a clear performance improvement, the work is likely to be adopted and to stimulate further research on integrating lexical ontologies with contextual embeddings, though the evaluation is limited to a single English PP dataset so broader impact is promising but somewhat scoped. | B(4): The paper tackles the well-known PP attachment challenge and introduces a general method—context-sensitive token embeddings grounded in WordNet synsets—that yields a substantial empirical gain (5.4 percentage points, 34.4% relative error reduction) over standard type-level embeddings. By integrating the approach into BiLSTM encoders, demonstrating clear improvement on a standard dataset, and releasing Keras layers, the work is likely to influence how researchers incorporate ontologies into contextual embeddings and to be adopted or extended in related tasks. While not necessarily transformative across all of NLP, it represents a strong, broadly applicable contribution that opens meaningful new directions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles the important problem of PP attachment disambiguation and proposes a concrete method—WordNet-grounded context-sensitive token embeddings—integrated into a BiLSTM+MLP model. Empirically it yields a substantial gain (5.4 percentage points, a 34.4% relative error reduction) over standard type-level embeddings on the Belinkov et al. dataset, showing that ontology grounding can materially improve this syntactic task. By releasing Keras layers and demonstrating a clear performance improvement, the work is likely to be adopted and to stimulate further research on integrating lexical ontologies with contextual embeddings, though the evaluation is limited to a single English PP dataset so broader impact is promising but somewhat scoped.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles the well-known PP attachment challenge and introduces a general method—context-sensitive token embeddings grounded in WordNet synsets—that yields a substantial empirical gain (5.4 percentage points, 34.4% relative error reduction) over standard type-level embeddings. By integrating the approach into BiLSTM encoders, demonstrating clear improvement on a standard dataset, and releasing Keras layers, the work is likely to influence how researchers incorporate ontologies into contextual embeddings and to be adopted or extended in related tasks. While not necessarily transformative across all of NLP, it represents a strong, broadly applicable contribution that opens meaningful new directions.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against reasonable and relevant baselines (LSTM-PP with GloVe, GloVe-retrofit, and the HPCD system from Belinkov et al. 2014) on the same PP-attachment dataset with the same accuracy/UAS metrics, and reports hyperparameters and code to support fairness. Related work coverage is described as thorough. However, the summary shows no acknowledgement of limitations or whether any prior methods perform comparably or better, and it appears to omit comparisons to more recent/contextualized embedding approaches (potentially a minor omission). | B(4): The paper compares against appropriate baselines (LSTM-PP with GloVe, GloVe-retrofitted, and the best standalone HPCD system from Belinkov et al. (2014)) and uses the same PP-attachment dataset and accuracy/UAS metrics, so comparisons appear fair. Differences are quantified (a 5.4 percentage point gain) and related work coverage is described as thorough. However, the paper does not state limitations or explicitly acknowledge cases where prior methods perform comparably, and it may omit more recent neural baselines, so the comparison is strong but not fully comprehensive.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against reasonable and relevant baselines (LSTM-PP with GloVe, GloVe-retrofit, and the HPCD system from Belinkov et al. 2014) on the same PP-attachment dataset with the same accuracy/UAS metrics, and reports hyperparameters and code to support fairness. APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(5): This paper sits squarely within top NLP/ML venues: it advances representation learning by proposing WordNet-grounded, context-sensitive token embeddings and applies them to the core NLP problem of PP attachment. The methods and evaluation follow community standards (bi-LSTM encoder, MLP scorer, comparison to strong baselines including HPCD and GloVe-retro, reporting of hyperparameters, and code release) and demonstrate a meaningful empirical gain (5.4 percentage points, 34.4% relative error reduction). Related work coverage is thorough and the task, dataset, and metrics are appropriate for the venue, making this a strong fit. | B(4): The paper addresses a core NLP/ML topic—context-sensitive, ontology-grounded token embeddings—and applies them to a canonical syntactic disambiguation task (PP attachment) using standard architectures (bi-LSTM + MLP). It evaluates on a standard dataset (Belinkov et al. 2014), compares against appropriate baselines (GloVe, retrofitted GloVe, HPCD), reports accuracy/UAS and substantial gains (5.4 percentage points, 34.4% relative error reduction), and provides code and hyperparameters. This fits the audience and methodological expectations of a top venue, though the empirical evaluation is fairly narrowly focused on PP attachment, which is a minor scope concern for the broadest ML audience.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized and follows a logical flow from motivation and method to experiments and conclusion; the main contributions (OntoLSTM token representation, integration with bi-LSTMs, and empirical gains) are stated early and clearly. The methodology and modeling choices are described concretely (WordNet synset embeddings, context-sensitive distributions, expected synset embeddings feeding a bi-LSTM and MLP), suggesting consistent notation and clear definition of components. Experimental presentation is informative—datasets, baselines, metrics, and a precise accuracy improvement are reported—and hyperparameters and code availability aid reproducibility. Overall writing quality is good, with only minor presentation polish needed to reach an exceptional level. | B(4): The paper appears well-organized with a clear flow from method to experiments (the proposed OntoLSTM-PP model, datasets, baselines, and evaluation are all described). Main contributions are stated early and clearly, and the writeup supports reproducibility (code available, hyperparameters reported) with thorough related-work coverage. Notation and methodology are presented coherently in the summary and overall writing quality is described as good; however, the summary does not report on figures/tables so their effectiveness cannot be judged from the provided information.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and empirically supported: OntoLSTM-PP uses context-sensitive distributions over WordNet synsets with expected synset embeddings and achieves a 5.4 percentage-point absolute improvement (34.4% relative error reduction) on the Belinkov et al. (2014) PP-attachment test set compared to strong LSTM and retrofitted-GloVe baselines and an HPCD prior system. The proposed modeling approach (soft-attention over synsets, synset embeddings initialized via AutoExtend on GloVe, bi-LSTM encoder + MLP scorer) is technically coherent and no mathematical inconsistencies are evident from the summary. Methodology is largely sound—appropriate baselines, dataset sizes, and hyperparameter reporting—but the summary does not mention statistical significance testing or explicit discussion of assumptions/limitations, which are minor gaps that prevent a top score. | B(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by experiments: OntoLSTM-PP (context-sensitive expected synset embeddings grounded in WordNet) outperforms LSTM baselines and the HPCD system on the Belinkov et al. PP attachment dataset, with reported hyperparameters and released code. The proposed modeling (soft-attention over synsets to form expected synset embeddings, fed into BiLSTM+MLP) is technically sound and no incorrect mathematical derivations are indicated in the summary. A minor methodological omission is the absence of reported statistical significance testing and no stated limitations, but these do not appear to undermine the reported empirical improvements.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and empirically supported: OntoLSTM-PP uses context-sensitive distributions over WordNet synsets with expected synset embeddings and achieves a 5.4 percentage-point absolute improvement (34.4% relative error reduction) on the Belinkov et al. (2014) PP-attachment test set compared to strong LSTM and retrofitted-GloVe baselines and an HPCD prior system. The proposed modeling approach (soft-attention over synsets, synset embeddings initialized via AutoExtend on GloVe, bi-LSTM encoder + MLP scorer) is technically coherent and no mathematical inconsistencies are evident from the summary. Methodology is largely sound—appropriate baselines, dataset sizes, and hyperparameter reporting—but the summary does not mention statistical significance testing or explicit discussion of assumptions/limitations, which are minor gaps that prevent a top score.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by experiments: OntoLSTM-PP (context-sensitive expected synset embeddings grounded in WordNet) outperforms LSTM baselines and the HPCD system on the Belinkov et al. PP attachment dataset, with reported hyperparameters and released code. The proposed modeling (soft-attention over synsets to form expected synset embeddings, fed into BiLSTM+MLP) is technically sound and no incorrect mathematical derivations are indicated in the summary. A minor methodological omission is the absence of reported statistical significance testing and no stated limitations, but these do not appear to undermine the reported empirical improvements.

main_claims: Propose a WordNet-grounded representation that models a word token as a context-sensitive probability distribution over WordNet concepts (synsets) and uses the expected synset embedding as the token embedding.
Integrate the proposed context-sensitive token embeddings with bidirectional LSTM encoders and an MLP scoring function to predict prepositional phrase (PP) attachments.
Show that the proposed OntoLSTM-PP model outperforms standard type-level embeddings for PP attachment, improving accuracy by 5.4 percentage points (a 34.4% relative reduction in errors).
Provide an implementation as Keras layers to facilitate reuse by other researchers.
_summary.methodology: Embed WordNet synsets (initialized via AutoExtend on 100-d GloVe) and for each token estimate a context-sensitive distribution over its grounded synsets (via a soft-attention-like mechanism using bi-LSTM context); use the expected synset embedding as the token input to a bi-LSTM encoder and score candidate heads by passing concatenated bi-LSTM outputs for head, preposition, and dependent through an MLP trained with cross-entropy.
datasets: English PP attachment dataset of Belinkov et al. (2014) (train: 33,359 examples; test: 1,951 examples)
baselines: LSTM-PP initialized with GloVe vectors retrofitted to WordNet (Faruqui et al., 2015) (GloVe-retro)
HPCD (full) — best standalone PP attachment system from Belinkov et al. (2014)
_summary.topic_area: NLP - PP attachment; context-sensitive word embeddings grounded in WordNet
